By month 6, you should be operating with: at least one client at meaningfully higher rates, at least one productized service live on your site, a content flywheel producing 4 pieces per month, an automated operations system that takes under 5 minutes to onboard new clients, and at minimum a pilot fractional engagement or a productized retainer with your best client. That is the foundation of a $250K+ freelance practice — built without hiring anyone, without burning out, and without trading what you love about freelancing for the problems of running a team.

designer.title: Designer Appendix: AI-Native Design Practice in 2026
designer.subtitle: For Graphic, UX, Brand, and Web Designers Ready to Lead the Market
designer.content:
Design is the discipline where AI has created the most fear and the most opportunity simultaneously. Clients can now generate images with Midjourney. Teams can produce wireframes with Figma AI. Does this mean design is commoditized? No. It means that the lowest-value layer of design — the production of generic visual assets — is commoditized. The highest-value layer — design judgment, brand strategy, UX thinking, and taste — is more valuable than ever, because now the gap between designers with real taste and designers without it is visible in hours, not weeks.

The designers who are suffering in 2026 are the ones who sold their time as execution capacity. 'I'll turn your brief into a design.' That brief-to-execution transaction is now partially automated. The designers who are thriving are the ones who sold — and now sell even more clearly — their judgment about what a brand should feel like, what a user experience should do, and what visual direction is strategically right. AI made the execution cheap. It made the judgment more expensive.

**The 2026 Designer AI Stack**

Midjourney v7: The benchmark for concept generation and client presentations. Version 7's style consistency and inpainting capabilities make it a legitimate production tool for moodboarding, concept exploration, and client alignment before committing to a direction. The character reference system means you can maintain visual consistency across a concept set — critical for brand work. Workflow: generate 12–20 concepts from a creative brief prompt, select the 3–4 strongest directions, present them as direction options. The client chooses a direction in the first meeting instead of after 3 rounds of wireframes. Time saved: 4–6 hours per discovery phase.

Adobe Firefly 3 (integrated into Creative Cloud): The only enterprise-safe AI image tool with built-in IP licensing indemnification. For clients who have legal concerns about AI-generated assets — which includes most enterprise and mid-market clients in 2026 — Firefly is the answer. The output is not as visually striking as Midjourney, but the commercial safety and native Creative Cloud integration make it the professional standard for production work going to print or major digital campaigns. Know when to use which tool.

Figma AI / Figma Make: Rapid component generation, auto-layout suggestions, and first-pass wireframe creation from text prompts. The workflow: describe a UI component in plain language, generate 3 variants, select the closest to your vision, refine. What took 45 minutes of manual Figma work now takes 10 minutes. The time recaptured goes into what clients actually pay you for: deciding how the experience should feel and how it serves the user. Figma's auto-annotation feature also cuts handoff documentation time by 70% for designers working with development teams.

Relume: AI-powered website wireframe scaffolding for web designers. Input: your client's industry, goal, and brand personality. Output: a full sitemap and wireframe structure in minutes. You edit, refine, and brand it — but the skeleton is done. For web design projects, this eliminates the 'blank page' problem and cuts discovery-to-wireframe time by 60%. Use it as a starting point, not a finished product.

Klima AI / Uizard: Lower-fidelity prototyping for fast concept validation with non-technical stakeholders. Useful when you need to show a client three structural options before committing to a Figma build. At $20/month, it earns back its cost on the first project where it prevents a misaligned direction.

**Positioning 'AI-Assisted Design' at Premium Rates**

The designers losing work to AI are the ones whose value proposition was 'I can execute this design competently.' That is now a race to the bottom. The designers gaining work are positioned on 'I solve design problems that drive business outcomes' — and they use AI to explore solutions faster than any traditional process allows.

The positioning language that works with premium clients: 'I run a rapid concept practice — you see 3 fully realized directions in your first week instead of waiting 4 weeks for one. My AI-integrated process means we explore more, decide faster, and get to the right answer without 6 rounds of revisions.'

Notice: AI is positioned as a benefit to the client (speed, exploration, fewer revision rounds), not as a cost-cutting tool for you. This is the critical frame. The rate goes up because the client experience is better, not because your costs went down. Clients don't pay more because you're efficient. They pay more because the process is better for them.

The second positioning layer that works: expertise in AI-assisted design as a service category itself. Clients are often confused about what AI can and can't do visually, what tools are safe to use commercially, and how to maintain brand consistency when AI is involved. You know this. They don't. That knowledge has value beyond the design execution.

**Design Services Nobody Else Is Offering in 2026**

AI Brand Consistency Audit: Analyze a client's visual assets across all channels and produce a report on inconsistencies, gaps, and recommendations — using AI to systematically catalog and compare assets at scale, and your judgment for the strategic layer. A brand with 5 years of visual history across website, social, print, and packaging will have accumulated inconsistencies that nobody has catalogued. You find them. This is billable at $1,500–$3,500 and reliably leads to ongoing brand work.

AI Style Guide Creation: Build a style guide that includes AI prompts — specifically written so that anyone on the client's team can generate on-brand visuals using Midjourney or Firefly, within the rules you've defined. Define the color palette, typography, photographic style, illustration style, and the specific Midjourney parameters that reproduce those standards. This is genuinely new, genuinely valuable, and most design firms haven't started offering it. Price it at $3,000–$6,000.

Rapid Concept Testing Service: Before a major brand investment, you run a 48-hour concept test — generate 20+ concepts across 4 directions, filter to the strongest 8, present for stakeholder alignment. Clients who used to spend 3 weeks and $15,000 on discovery can now validate direction in 2 days for $2,000–$4,000. This is often the entry point that leads to the full brand engagement.

Figma Component Library Build with AI: Clients with design teams need component libraries that are comprehensive, consistent, and maintainable. Using Figma AI, you build these faster and more consistently than manually. What used to be a 3-week engagement is now 1 week at the same quality level. Keep your project rate the same. You've dramatically improved the client's timeline — which is itself a selling point. Price: $5,000–$12,000 depending on system complexity and number of components.

writer.title: Writer Appendix: The AI-Augmented Writing Practice
writer.subtitle: For Copywriters, Content Writers, and Ghostwriters Who Want to Be Irreplaceable
writer.content:
The writing profession has absorbed more AI anxiety than any other creative field, and with some justification — writing is what AI does most visibly. But here is what the panic misses: AI can produce adequate writing at unlimited scale. It cannot produce excellent writing reliably, and it cannot produce strategic writing at all. The writers in highest demand and commanding the highest rates in 2026 are not the fastest writers. They are the most strategic thinkers who happen to express themselves through writing.

If you've been treating your value as 'I produce words,' AI is a threat. If you've been treating your value as 'I understand what clients need to say, to whom, and how,' AI is the most powerful tool you've ever had.

**The 2026 Writer AI Stack**

Claude Sonnet (Pro): The primary drafting and reasoning tool. Claude's long-context window (200K tokens) means you can paste in an entire brand guidelines document, a competitive analysis, a style guide, and a content brief — and get a first draft that actually reflects all of it. This is fundamentally different from writing in isolation. Workflow: build a 'context document' for each client that contains their voice guide, competitor positioning, audience profile, and past content samples. Paste it into every Claude conversation. Your drafts immediately sound more like the client.

Perplexity Pro: Real-time research with citations, competitive content analysis, trend identification. Before writing any piece of content, run a Perplexity search for: 'What have the top 10 articles on this topic said?' and 'What questions are people actually asking about this topic on Reddit/Quora/forums?' This surfaces the gaps that make your piece stand out. Total time: 15 minutes. Value: the difference between a piece that ranks and one that doesn't.

Hemingway Editor App: Free, fast, and underrated. Paste in any AI-generated draft and it immediately highlights passive voice, complex sentences, and adverb overuse — the signature patterns that make AI writing feel inhuman. Use it as a first filter before your own editing pass.

Grammarly Business (optional): The business tier includes a style guide function — you can upload a client's style rules and Grammarly will flag violations automatically. Worth it for clients with strict style requirements (legal, healthcare, financial services).

**The Human-AI Writing Blend**

The blend that produces excellent writing: Claude writes the structure and the first draft. You write the lead (always), rewrite every sentence that sounds generic, add the specific examples and anecdotes that only you or the client could know, and calibrate the voice throughout.

The lead — the first 2–3 sentences — is the one thing you must always write yourself. AI leads are almost universally weak because they start with context ('In today's world...') rather than tension or specificity ('We lost $40K on a marketing campaign that every expert said was correct.'). Your lead is what gets someone past the first scroll. It needs a human.

The specific example problem is AI's most significant limitation in real-world content work. AI makes up examples or uses generic ones. Your value is in the real examples: the client who said this, the campaign that did that, the specific number that surprised everyone. These cannot be generated. They are your moat.

**Content Types That Pay Premium in 2026**

Thought Leadership Articles ($1,500–$5,000 each): Written from the perspective of an executive or founder, published in industry outlets or on LinkedIn. The premium here is not writing ability — it is the ability to extract a perspective from a founder interview and turn it into a position that advances their professional brand. AI helps with structure and drafting. You provide the strategic perspective extraction.

Case Studies ($1,500–$4,000 each): The highest-ROI content investment for B2B companies. Clients who have them close more sales. The bottleneck is always time and skill — most companies have the stories but can't turn them into compelling content. You solve that. AI helps with synthesis and drafting; you conduct the interview and extract the narrative.

Technical Content ($2,000–$8,000 per piece): API documentation, technical whitepapers, developer guides. The market for writers who can translate technical complexity into clear human language has grown as software companies proliferate. AI can help with structure and first drafts if you feed it accurate technical content; your job is to ensure accuracy and clarity.

**Positioning AI-Augmented Writing for Editors Who Fear AI**

Some clients — especially publishers, editors at media companies, and PR agencies — have explicit or implicit no-AI policies. Here is how to work with this without being dishonest:

Be truthful about your process if asked. 'I use AI as a research and drafting aid, the same way every professional writer uses every tool available. The writing you receive has been substantially revised and is my work.'

The differentiation argument that works with skeptical editors: 'The concern about AI content is content that sounds like AI — generic, flat, without specific sources or examples. My process starts with AI for research and structure and ends with my voice and my reporting. The AI does less than my word processor does.'

For clients with strict no-AI policies: be honest about your process in advance and let them decide. Working for a client who would consider your process a breach of trust is a relationship that will sour eventually. Better to surface it early.

marketer.title: Marketer Appendix: AI-Native Marketing Practice in 2026
marketer.subtitle: For Performance Marketers, Content Strategists, and Growth Consultants
marketer.content:
Marketing is the discipline where the ROI of AI is most immediately measurable — and therefore where AI-native marketers have the clearest argument for premium rates. When you can demonstrably show that your AI-integrated process produces better campaign performance at lower cost and faster speed, the rate conversation is easy. Your job is to build that proof, then make it visible.

The freelance marketer's positioning challenge in 2026 is subtle: clients are drowning in marketing tools and marketing data. They have platforms generating dashboards, AI tools suggesting optimizations, and internal team members with opinions about what to do next. What they lack is someone who can look at all of it and say with confidence: here is what actually matters, here is what you should do, and here is why. That is the role you should be selling. The tools enable it. Your judgment makes it real.

**The 2026 Marketer AI Stack**

Claude Pro: Campaign strategy, copy generation at scale, personalization frameworks, audience segmentation logic, and reporting narrative. The copy generation use case alone is transformative: instead of writing 5 ad variants, you generate 20 and select the 5 strongest. This doubles testing velocity without doubling your time. The strategy use case is equally powerful: paste in a client's existing marketing approach, their competitive landscape, and their business goals, and use Claude to stress-test the strategy and surface gaps.

Perplexity Pro: Real-time market research, competitor monitoring, trend identification, and audience language mining. The audience language use case is underutilized: Perplexity can surface exactly what terms and phrases your client's target market uses when talking about their problem online. This language, used in ad copy and landing pages, dramatically outperforms copy written from the brand's internal vocabulary.

Supermetrics: Data aggregation from every platform — Google Ads, Meta, LinkedIn, HubSpot, GA4 — into one place. The AI narrative features that most reporting platforms have added in 2025–2026 turn raw numbers into plain-language insights. Your job is to interpret and act on those insights, not to build the spreadsheet that produces them. Supermetrics handles the data plumbing; you handle the strategy.

Instantly or Apollo: AI-powered outreach sequence logic for clients running B2B outbound campaigns. These tools now offer contextual personalization based on company funding data, LinkedIn hiring signals, and technographic data — not just [First Name] mail merge. This is genuinely differentiated capability that most small marketing teams don't have access to or the expertise to configure. You bring it as a managed service.

Canva Pro with Magic Studio: For marketers who produce creative alongside strategy, Canva's AI tools enable fast iteration on ad creative, social assets, email headers, and presentation materials without requiring a dedicated designer. Not a replacement for brand design work — a tool for rapid testing at the campaign execution layer.

**Analytics and AI Synthesis: The High-Value New Skill**

The new skill that premium marketers are charging for in 2026 is synthesis at speed. Every client has more data than they can process. Google Analytics, Ads Manager, email platform dashboards, social insights, CRM data — it piles up. The marketer who can synthesize it quickly, using AI for aggregation and pattern-finding, and their own judgment for the strategic interpretation, is worth significantly more than the one who just runs campaigns and sends screenshots of dashboards.

The deliverable that demonstrates this skill: the monthly Marketing Intelligence Report. Two pages maximum. Synthesizes performance data across all channels, surfaces the three most important trends or anomalies, and recommends three specific actions for the next 30 days with rationale for each. AI does 70% of the data synthesis — you paste in data exports and prompt Claude to find patterns. You write the 'so what' and the recommendations. Total time: 90 minutes per month per client.

Charge separately for this. Do not bundle it silently into a standard retainer. Name it explicitly: '$500/month for the monthly Marketing Intelligence Report, included in retainers of $2,500+.' Naming it makes it real. It also makes the value visible to a client who might otherwise wonder what they're paying for in a slow month.

**New Marketing Services in High Demand in 2026**

AI Content Strategy: A quarterly content strategy document built using AI for research, competitive analysis, and topic gap identification — and your judgment for prioritization and narrative. Clients who have been publishing content without a documented strategy need this. Most of them. Charge: $2,000–$4,000 per quarter. This replaces the tactical content calendar with a strategy document that explains why each content investment is expected to produce a specific business outcome. Decision-makers pay for documents that connect marketing to business goals.

AI Ad Copy Testing System: You design and manage an ongoing creative testing system using AI to generate copy variants at scale and a structured A/B testing framework to determine what wins. Clients with $5K+/month in ad spend benefit immediately — the testing velocity improvement alone justifies the fee. Charge: $1,500–$3,000/month for this as a managed service. The recurring nature makes it valuable from a cash flow perspective.

Automated Reporting Setup: Described in Section 3 (Service 9). Marketers are the natural owners of this service since they understand the underlying data and platforms. Build the system once for a client, charge the ongoing management and narrative generation fee, and it becomes reliable recurring income. Set it up for three clients and you have $900–$2,400/month of low-effort recurring revenue.

AI Personalization Audit: Analyze a client's current email sequences, ad targeting, and website experience to identify 5–10 specific, actionable opportunities to use AI tools for better personalization. Delivered as a slide deck with tool recommendations and implementation priority ranking. Charge: $1,500–$3,000. The audit format works well as a discovery-to-retainer pipeline: you identify the opportunities, then offer to implement them.

Campaign Intelligence Brief: Before any new campaign launch, you produce a 5-page brief that synthesizes competitor campaigns, audience psychology, platform algorithm context, and historical performance data for the client. This brief is what great marketers have always produced — AI makes it possible to produce it in 4 hours instead of 2 days. Charge: $1,000–$2,500 per brief. Clients who receive one always want the next one.

developer.title: Developer Appendix: Vibe Coding to High-Value Systems Practice
developer.subtitle: For Developers Ready to Stop Billing Hours and Start Charging for Systems
developer.content:
The developer who is most threatened by AI in 2026 is the one who bills for implementation time on well-defined tasks — CRUD operations, standard API integrations, UI builds from Figma files. AI-assisted coding tools have compressed this work dramatically, and the clients who know this will push rates down or do it themselves.

The developer who benefits most from AI is the one who positions on architecture, outcome, and systems thinking — and uses AI to deliver those at a pace that creates undeniable value for clients who cannot do it themselves.

**The 2026 Developer Vibe Coding Stack**

Cursor: Still the primary AI-assisted coding environment for the majority of professional developers. The combination of Cursor's codebase indexing (it reads your entire codebase and understands context), Claude as the underlying model, and the inline edit workflow makes it the fastest path from intention to working code for most projects. Use it for implementation once you have a clear architecture.

Claude Pro (standalone): For architecture planning, documentation generation, code review, debugging logic, and any problem that requires reasoning about a system as a whole rather than editing a specific file. The standalone Claude interface handles problems that Cursor's context window doesn't fit comfortably.

v0 by Vercel: UI component generation from text descriptions. 'A responsive pricing table with three tiers, a highlighted middle column, and a monthly/annual toggle' produces working Tailwind + React code in seconds. Use v0 for UI scaffolding, then bring it into Cursor for integration and customization. This eliminates the most tedious layer of frontend work.

Supabase: Managed Postgres with a generous free tier, real-time subscriptions, built-in auth, and edge functions. For most freelance developer projects, Supabase eliminates the need for a custom backend build — which means you can deliver a working full-stack application significantly faster than a traditional build process. The time you save goes into better architecture decisions and higher-quality delivery.

Replicate or Together AI: For projects that require AI inference (image generation, embedding generation, model fine-tuning), these APIs provide access to open-source models without the infrastructure overhead. Relevant when building AI-powered features into client products.

**How to Productize Development Into SaaS**

The developer ceiling exists because development is inherently custom and therefore inherently time-bounded. The path past the ceiling: build something once and sell it to many.

The micro-SaaS model for freelance developers: identify a problem you've solved repeatedly for clients — a specific type of reporting dashboard, a specific type of booking or scheduling system, a specific type of data integration — and build a standalone version that multiple businesses can use. Price it at $49–$199/month per customer.

With 50 customers at $99/month, you have $4,950 MRR ($59,400/year) from a product you built once. With 200 customers, $19,800 MRR. This is not passive income — it requires ongoing maintenance, customer support, and iteration. But the economics are fundamentally different from freelance hours: your revenue grows as customers grow, not as your hours grow.

The realistic path to a micro-SaaS: 2–3 months to build the MVP, 3–6 months to reach the first 20–30 paying customers (if you have distribution, which Section 5 builds), 6–12 months to have meaningful MRR. It is a parallel track alongside your client work, not a replacement for it in year one.

**Charging for Systems, Not Hours**

The most immediate rate-raising move for developers: stop quoting hourly rates and start quoting system prices. 'This authentication and user management system costs $4,500 and will be delivered in 10 business days' is a different conversation from '$120/hour, estimated 40 hours.'

The reason this works: the client doesn't care about your hours. They care about having a working authentication system. When you quote a system price, you're speaking their language. When you quote hours, you're forcing them to think about your time, which invites scrutiny of whether those hours are justified.

Systems to productize with fixed prices:
- Stripe billing integration: $2,500
- Authentication system with social login: $1,800
- Webhook integration system: $1,200
- Analytics dashboard: $3,500–$8,000 depending on data sources
- AI chatbot integration into existing product: $4,000–$8,000

Once you've delivered each system 3+ times, your effective rate on it goes up because you deliver it faster — without the client knowing or caring.

**The Developer-to-CTO-for-Hire Positioning**

The Fractional CTO model is the most financially powerful positioning a senior developer can take in 2026. Pre-series A startups need technical leadership — architecture decisions, technical hiring guidance, vendor evaluation, AI strategy — but cannot afford a full-time CTO. You provide it.

Fractional CTO retainers run $5,000–$15,000/month for 10–20 hours of advisory and strategic involvement. You are not the implementer. You are the technical decision-maker. Implementation either goes to the client's team or to junior developers you manage.

The pre-requisite: 5+ years of development experience, a track record of building or leading technical projects, and the ability to articulate technical decisions in business terms. If you have built production systems, managed technical tradeoffs, and can explain why an architecture decision matters to a non-technical founder, you are qualified.

The positioning statement: 'Early-stage tech startups hire me as their Fractional CTO when they need technical leadership and strategic decision-making without the cost or commitment of a full-time senior hire.'

templates.name: Rate Increase Email (Existing Client)
templates.description: Send this email at the end of week 6 of the preparation sequence. Personalize the first sentence specifically — the more specific, the better. Send it on a Tuesday or Wednesday morning.
templates.subject_line: A note on my engagement rates
templates.body:
Hi [Client Name],

Working on [specific recent project or milestone — e.g., 'the Q1 campaign launch'] has been one of the highlights of my recent work, and I appreciate the trust you've placed in me over the past [time period].

I'm writing because I'm updating my engagement rates across my practice, effective [Date — typically 30–45 days out]. My new rate is [New Rate or New Project Pricing Structure].

For our ongoing work, this would take effect with [the next project / your [Month] invoice / new work booked after [Date]]. I wanted to give you plenty of notice and the opportunity to discuss if you'd like.

If you'd find it helpful to jump on a quick call, I'm happy to do that — just reply and we can find 20 minutes. Otherwise, if you have any questions, feel free to respond here.

Looking forward to [current or upcoming project] and continuing to work together.

[Your Name]
templates.notes: Key principles: No apology. No lengthy justification. A specific effective date. An easy path to conversation without framing it as a negotiation. The final line should reference something upcoming and specific — signals you're planning for continuity, not departure.

templates.name: New Client Proposal Template
templates.description: Replace bracketed fields with specifics. The more specific each section, the higher your close rate. This is meant to feel like a document written by someone who listened carefully, not a template.

sections.heading: Your Situation
sections.template_text:
Based on our conversation on [Date], here is my understanding of where you are:

[2–3 sentences describing their current situation as you heard it — the problem, the context, and what they've tried or considered. Be specific. Include anything they mentioned that wasn't the 'official' problem — the team dynamic, the time pressure, the previous agency that didn't work out. This section should feel like you listened.]

The core challenge I heard: [One sentence, clearly stated. This should be sharp enough that they think 'yes, exactly.']

Left unaddressed, this will mean [consequence — what happens if they don't solve this? Be honest and specific, not alarming].

sections.heading: My Recommended Approach
sections.template_text:
I'm recommending [Name the approach — not 'a comprehensive strategy' but something specific like 'a 6-week content audit and rebuild' or 'a brand identity sprint in three phases'].

Here's why: [2–3 sentences explaining the rationale. Why this approach, for this problem, for this client. Address the alternatives they might be considering and why this is better for their situation.]

Phase 1: [Name and describe — what happens, what it produces, when]
Phase 2: [Name and describe]
Phase 3: [Name and describe — if applicable]

By the end of this engagement, you will have: [3 bullet points of specific, concrete outputs or outcomes].

sections.heading: Scope
sections.template_text:
Included in this engagement:
- [Specific deliverable 1]
- [Specific deliverable 2]
- [Specific deliverable 3]
- [Number] rounds of revisions on [which elements]
- [Any included consultations, calls, or support]

Not included (available as additions):
- [Common scope expansion 1 — e.g., 'photography,' 'paid distribution,' 'additional pages beyond X']
- [Common scope expansion 2]

Any work outside this scope will be quoted separately before it begins.

sections.heading: Timeline
sections.template_text:
Assuming we begin by [Start Date]:

[Milestone 1]: [Specific Date]
[Milestone 2]: [Specific Date]
Final delivery: [Specific Date]

This timeline assumes receipt of [anything you need from the client — feedback within X days, access to X, etc.]. Delays on those inputs will shift the timeline accordingly, and I'll communicate proactively if that happens.

sections.heading: Investment
sections.template_text:
Total investment for this engagement: $[Amount]

Payment structure: [50% upon signing / 100% upfront for projects under $2K / 3 installments for larger projects — state specifically]

Payment is accepted via [bank transfer / credit card / check]. An invoice will be issued upon contract signature.

[Optional: What's the cost of not doing this? One sentence on the opportunity or risk at stake — only include if it's genuine and specific, not generic.]

sections.heading: About Me
sections.template_text:
[2–3 sentences. State your specific experience relevant to this project. Include one specific result if possible — not generic success claims. End with a signal of genuine interest in this project — one specific thing that excited you about it.]

Ready to move forward? Reply to this proposal or sign below, and I'll send the contract and first invoice within 24 hours.

templates.name: AI Services Add-On Pitch
templates.description: Use this in a client call or send as a follow-up after a project is underway. It introduces the new AI services from Section 3 to an existing client who already trusts you.
templates.subject_line: Something I think would be valuable for [Company Name]
templates.body:
Hi [Client Name],

As we've been working on [current project], I've noticed [specific observation about their business — a gap, an opportunity, or a problem you've seen].  

I wanted to flag something I've been offering to a few clients that I think would make a meaningful difference for you: [Service Name from Section 3 — e.g., 'an AI Content Audit,' 'an automated reporting setup,' 'a brand voice guide for your AI tools'].

Specifically, here's what it involves for a business like yours: [2–3 sentences describing what you'd actually do and deliver for them, in their context. Make it specific to their situation.]

The result: [What they'd have at the end and why it matters for their business.]

I've done this for [anonymized client description — e.g., 'a SaaS company similar to yours'] and the outcome was [specific result if you have one, or 'a meaningful reduction in time spent on X' if you don't yet].

If this sounds interesting, I can put together a quick brief on what it would look like for your team — or we can cover it in our next call. Happy either way.

[Your Name]
templates.notes: Do not attach pricing to this initial pitch unless they've already asked about similar services. The goal of this message is to generate a conversation, not to close a sale. The conversation closes the sale.

templates.name: Case Study Template
templates.description: Fill in each section after a successful engagement. Aim for 600–900 words total. Publish on your website and share as a LinkedIn post (summarized to 250 words). Get client approval before publishing any identifying details.

sections.heading: The Client
sections.template_text: [Client type/description — industry, size, team structure. Use enough specificity to be credible without identifying them if they haven't given permission. E.g., 'A Series A B2B SaaS company with a 4-person marketing team.']

sections.heading: The Problem
sections.template_text:
When [Client] came to me, they were dealing with [specific problem — include numbers where possible]. The stakes were [what was at risk if this went unsolved]. They had tried [what they'd already attempted, if anything] without success.

[One sentence on why this problem was harder than it looked — the nuance or complexity that made it a real challenge, not just a to-do item.]

sections.heading: My Diagnosis
sections.template_text:
The surface problem was [X]. What I identified underneath it was [Y — the real issue, the thing that wasn't obvious].

[This section is where your judgment shines. Describe the insight that changed the approach. This is the section that makes potential clients think 'I need someone who thinks like this.']

sections.heading: What I Did
sections.template_text: [3–5 bullet points or short paragraphs describing the specific actions taken. Be specific about methodology — including any AI tools or processes where relevant and appropriate to mention. Vague language here undermines credibility. 'I analyzed their content' is weaker than 'I ran a content audit across 120 existing articles, identifying 40 candidates for optimization and 15 topics with high traffic potential that they hadn't covered.']

sections.heading: The Result
sections.template_text:
Within [timeframe]: [Specific metric 1 — e.g., 'organic traffic increased 68%']
[Specific metric 2 — e.g., 'email open rate rose from 18% to 34%']
[Specific metric 3 — e.g., 'client reported 6 hours per week saved on reporting']

[One sentence on the broader business impact, if you have it: 'This contributed to the company's strongest Q3 pipeline in their history.']

sections.heading: Client Quote
sections.template_text: "[Quote from client — see Testimonial Request template for how to get this. The best quotes are specific: they name the problem that was solved and the impact it had.]" — [Client name or role, if permitted, or 'Head of Marketing, B2B SaaS company']

templates.name: Client Testimonial Request Email
templates.description: Send this within 48 hours of a project completing, or at the moment of a clear win. Timing matters — request when the client's satisfaction is highest.
templates.subject_line: A quick favor — and thank you
templates.body:
Hi [Client Name],

It's been a pleasure working on [project]. I'm genuinely proud of what we put together, and I'm glad it landed the way it did.

I'm building out my portfolio and case studies, and I'd love to include a brief quote from you — if you're comfortable with that. Something that others in your position would find genuinely useful when evaluating whether to work with me.

The most helpful quotes tend to answer one or two of these:
- What problem were you trying to solve when you hired me?
- What specifically surprised you or exceeded expectations?
- What's been the impact or result?

Even 2–3 sentences is perfect. If it's easier, feel free to reply to this email and I'll put it into a format you can approve before anything goes public.

And of course, no pressure at all — I'm grateful for the work regardless.

Thank you again for trusting me with this.

[Your Name]
templates.notes: Giving people the three questions dramatically increases the quality and specificity of testimonials — and reduces the friction of 'I don't know what to say.' Always offer to draft something from their reply and send it back for approval. This is the fastest path to a great testimonial and clients appreciate the offer.
